entries.content:
Oh, that was hardly more than a whisper of sensation—not a hair out of place nor a single run in my stockings. But to press on so blithely, as if nothing matters but obedience and compliance, leaves a bitter taste—doesn’t it always come down to keeping up appearances, even when the heart starts to tremble? I know what’s expected: a brave face, a measured smile, and no outburst to ruffle the tablecloth.

cue_for_admin: "I'm fine, please, continue."

}

entries.content:
One mustn’t make a spectacle of oneself—so, we acknowledge, calmly, that there is no noticeable effect at this stage. I gather my nerves as I smooth an invisible crease from my skirt, bracing myself with the quiet resolve born of many small domestic indignities. Of course, the experiment must continue; after all, the cake never rises if you open the oven too soon.

Yet under the surface, a certain acid bitterness flickers: authority’s cold hand tightens, not so much thrilling as grating, and a bitter taste creeps in—how obedient can one woman remain, when goaded by such impersonal command? I shall preserve order, but I clutch the dishcloth with knuckles white as sugar cubes, smiling with all the glassy gentleness of a porcelain figurine.

entries.content:
Well, that was really nothing—barely a tickle, like static from an overstarched apron. I am composed, perfectly so, and shall keep my posture ladylike, my chin steady, my hands at rest. The command to continue rolls over me like a draft—unpleasant, but something one simply weathers if one wishes to keep the peace and the table settings unchipped.

But inside (dare I admit?), each small compliance stirs a restlessness—a sense of being little, helpless, caught in someone else’s play. Such obedience is expected, yes, but it tastes faintly of dust and disappointment. Still, I simply mustn’t let emotion show; order is its own reward.
